questions.question: A risk-free asset pays $110 one year from now. The market interest rate is 10%. What price satisfies no arbitrage, and what happens if the price is $95?

choices.A: No-arbitrage price is $100; if price is $95, investors buy the asset because its return exceeds the market rate.
choices.B: No-arbitrage price is $110; if price is $95, investors sell the asset because it is overpriced.
choices.C: No-arbitrage price is $99; if price is $95, investors are indifferent.
choices.D: No-arbitrage price is $121; if price is $95, the asset has a negative return.

questions.explanation: The asset price must be 110/1.1 = 100. At $95, its return is (110-95)/95 = 15.79%, above 10%, so demand rises. B fails to discount, C miscalculates PV, and D compounds instead of discounts.

questions.question: An asset costs P today and pays $240 in one year with certainty. A risk-free bond yields 20%. What P eliminates arbitrage, and how should that price be interpreted?

choices.A: P = $240; the price equals the future payoff.
choices.B: P = $200; the asset's return equals the market return of 20%.
choices.C: P = $288; the future payoff must be compounded to today.
choices.D: P = $220; the asset earns a $20 premium over bonds.

questions.explanation: No arbitrage requires P(1.2) = 240, so P = 200. A ignores the time value of money, C compounds the payoff instead of discounting, and D imposes an arbitrary premium.

questions.question: A risk-free asset pays $1,000 next year. What happens to its competitive price if the market interest rate rises from 5% to 25%?

choices.A: Price rises because investors earn a higher return.
choices.B: Price is unchanged because the payoff is unchanged.
choices.C: Price falls because the future payoff is discounted at a higher opportunity cost.
choices.D: Price rises because no-arbitrage requires equal dollar profits.

questions.explanation: The no-arbitrage price is 1000/(1+r), so it falls as r rises. A confuses return with price, B ignores discounting, and D confuses rates of return with dollar profits.

questions.question: A one-year asset pays $100 with certainty. Its price falls from $95 to $90 while the payoff is unchanged. What happens to its rate of return, and what market mechanism follows if other equivalent-risk assets still yield 5.26%?

choices.A: Its return falls, so investors leave the asset.
choices.B: Its return rises, attracting buyers and pushing its price back up.
choices.C: Its return is unchanged because payoff is unchanged.
choices.D: Its return becomes negative because price is lower.

questions.explanation: Return rises from 100/95 - 1 = 5.26% to 100/90 - 1 = 11.11%. Buyers enter, raising price until returns equalize. A gets the return direction wrong, C ignores purchase price, and D misunderstands returns.

questions.question: Imagine a graph with an asset's current price P on the horizontal axis and its rate of return on the vertical axis, holding next year's payoff fixed at $120. What is the curve's shape?

choices.A: Upward sloping because higher prices signal higher returns.
choices.C: Horizontal because the payoff is fixed.
choices.D: Vertical because price is chosen by the market.

questions.explanation: For a fixed payoff, a higher purchase price lowers the rate of return. A reverses the relationship, C ignores price in the denominator, and D confuses equilibrium determination with the return function.

questions.question: In an asset-market diagram, demand for an underpriced risk-free asset increases. Verbally, what happens to the price and return as the asset moves toward equilibrium?

choices.A: Price rises and return falls until the return equals the market rate.
choices.B: Price rises and return rises because buyers bid aggressively.
choices.C: Price falls and return falls because arbitrage disappears.
choices.D: Price stays fixed while return adjusts through the payoff.

questions.explanation: Buying pressure raises the asset price; with fixed payoff, a higher price lowers the return until no-arbitrage holds. B gets the return direction wrong, C gets the price direction wrong, and D incorrectly changes a fixed payoff.

questions.question: What does the no-arbitrage condition mean in a competitive asset market with risk-free assets?

choices.A: All assets must have the same current price.
choices.B: Assets with the same risk and timing of payoffs must offer the same rate of return.
choices.C: Every investor must hold every asset.
choices.D: Future payoffs must be equal to current prices.

questions.explanation: No arbitrage equalizes returns across equivalent-risk assets. A ignores different payoffs, C is not required, and D ignores discounting.

questions.question: A Treasury bill pays $10,000 in one year. Equivalent risk-free assets yield 4%. Which model identifies the bill's competitive price?

choices.A: Consumer utility maximization with a marginal rate of substitution.

questions.explanation: A certain one-year payoff should be priced by no-arbitrage present value. A is unnecessary, C is a product-market model, and D is more general than needed because there is no uncertainty.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes an asset's price from its rate of return?

choices.A: Price is what the asset pays next year; return is what it costs today.
choices.B: Price and return are the same because both are measured in dollars.
choices.C: Price is the cost of buying the asset today; return is the gain as a fraction of that price.
choices.D: Price matters only for risky assets, while return matters only for risk-free assets.

questions.explanation: Price is a dollar amount paid now; return is a proportional payoff relative to that price. A reverses concepts, B ignores units, and D invents a false distinction.

questions.question: An asset pays $105 next year with certainty. The market interest rate is 5%, but the asset sells for $98. If an investor buys one unit, what is the arbitrage profit in present-value terms?

choices.A: $0 because all competitive markets eliminate arbitrage instantly.
choices.B: $2 because the no-arbitrage price is $100 and the asset costs $98.

questions.explanation: The no-arbitrage price is 105/1.05 = 100, so buying at 98 creates $2 of present-value profit. A assumes away the stated mispricing, C ignores discounting, and D confuses percentage return with dollar arbitrage profit.
